programs.name: Statewide Medicaid Managed Care Long Term Care Program
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, this program covers home care, adult day care, and nursing home services through Medicaid.

localEntities.name: Local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)
Managed Care Plans selected via enrollment broker

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or taking medications, Florida's Medicaid Long Term Care program can cover the full cost of these services at home or in a nursing facility. Because this program coordinates all long-term care through a single Managed Care Organization, your parent won't need to juggle multiple providers — one team manages everything from personal care to medical equipment.

The program covers home-delivered meals, personal care services, adult day care, respite care (giving you breaks), medical equipment, skilled nursing visits, and nursing home care when needed. Your parent can even hire family members as paid caregivers in some cases. However, your parent must meet strict income and asset limits (similar to regular Medicaid) and pass a clinical assessment proving they need Nursing Facility Level of Care.

summary: Meet Medicaid income limits (around 88% of Federal Poverty Level)
Need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medications
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other basic activities)
otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicaid financially

applicationGuide.summary: Start by calling the Elder Helpline at 1-800-963-5337 for screening, then complete separate financial and medical eligibility processes.
applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies due to multi-step process involving screening, financial assessment, and clinical evaluation
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible wait list before clinical assessment
applicationGuide.tip: Start the process early — you'll need to work with three different agencies (ADRC for screening, DCF for financial eligibility, and DOEA for medical assessment).

steps.title: Initial screening
steps.description: Contact your local Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC) or call Elder Helpline 1-800-963-5337 to begin screening process.

steps.title: Financial eligibility
steps.description: Apply for Medicaid financial eligibility through Department of Children and Families (DCF) with income and asset documentation.

steps.title: Medical assessment
steps.description: Complete clinical assessment with DOEA CARES unit to determine if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care using form AHCA 5000-3008.

steps.title: Plan enrollment
steps.description: Choose a Managed Care Organization (MCO) plan through the enrollment broker after approval.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records documenting need for daily assistance
Recent hospital or nursing home discharge summaries

contentSections.text: This program involves three separate agencies: ADRC for screening, DCF for financial approval, and DOEA for medical assessment. Delays at any step can slow the entire process, and there may be a waitlist before the clinical assessment.

The program doesn't cover room and board costs in assisted living facilities — only the care services themselves. If your parent lives in assisted living, they'll still need to pay rent and meals separately.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit as long as they live there or plan to return home.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to take care of Dad through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, in some cases. The program offers consumer-directed options that allow your parent to hire family members as paid caregivers, but this requires approval from the Managed Care Organization.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and regular nursing home Medicaid?
faqs.answer: This program focuses on keeping your parent at home or in the community rather than in a nursing facility. It coordinates all long-term care services through one Managed Care Organization instead of separate providers.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits follow standard Florida Medicaid rules (around 88% of Federal Poverty Level). Contact DCF to determine exact limits for your parent's situation, as there may be deductions or special rules that apply.

faqs.question: How long does the clinical assessment take?
faqs.answer: The CARES unit assessment timing varies and there may be a waitlist. The assessment determines if your parent needs Nursing Facility Level of Care using specific clinical criteria.

faqs.question: What happens if we disagree with the eligibility decision?
faqs.answer: You can request a Fair Hearing if you disagree with any screening or eligibility determination. The program must provide information on how to request this appeal.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All medical care, adult day programs, home care, and transportation coordinated by one team if your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home.

localEntities.name: Miami Jewish Health Systems (Miami-Dade and Broward)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with three or more daily activities like bathing, dressing, or managing medications — but you want them to stay at home — PACE provides everything under one roof. Because PACE covers all Medicare and Medicaid benefits plus extras like transportation and home-delivered meals, your parent gets one care team instead of juggling separate doctors, home health agencies, and adult day programs.

Your parent must be 55+ and live in Miami-Dade or Broward counties. They need to qualify for nursing home level care but be able to live safely in the community with PACE support. Most PACE participants are eligible for both Medicare and Medicaid.

PACE replaces your parent's existing Medicare and Medicaid coverage — they cannot keep Medicare Advantage or other plans while enrolled. The interdisciplinary team creates a customized care plan covering everything from prescription drugs to recreational therapy to emergency hospital care.

summary: Lives in Miami-Dade or Broward counties
Can live safely at home with support
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home level care — typically requires help with 3+ activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, or medication management. Certified by physician and state assessment.
otherRequirements: Able to live safely in community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, prescription drug plan, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Call for phone screening, then in-home visits complete paperwork and assessments — typically 1-3 months total.

steps.title: Call for pre-screening
steps.description: Phone screening determines if your parent meets basic PACE requirements and lives in service area.

steps.title: Schedule in-home visit
steps.description: PACE staff visit your parent's home to complete enrollment paperwork and initial assessment.

steps.title: Medical certification
steps.description: Primary care physician completes Form 3008 certifying your parent needs long-term care assistance.

steps.title: State assessment
steps.description: Florida Department of Elder Affairs conducts CARES assessment to confirm nursing home level of care need.

steps.title: Medicaid coordination
steps.description: If not already enrolled, PACE helps coordinate Medicaid enrollment through Department of Children and Families.

applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's primary care physician ready to complete Form 3008 — this medical certification is required and can slow the process if delayed.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits for Medicaid Eligibility
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits. PACE has no direct income limits, but Medicaid eligibility often required. Medicaid planning can help if over limits.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Medicaid
excluded: Burial funds up to reasonable amount

categories.name: Financial
items: Income statements (Social Security benefits verification letter)

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in Miami-Dade and Broward counties. Your parent must live in the designated service area to enroll.

PACE becomes your parent's sole provider for all Medicare and Medicaid covered services. They cannot keep Medicare Advantage or use other providers while enrolled — but they get comprehensive care coordination instead.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes and communities. The primary home is exempt from Medicaid asset limits, and PACE provides services to help your mom live safely at home.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: PACE itself has no income limits — only Medicaid does. A Medicaid planning attorney can help with strategies like qualified income trusts if your mom's income exceeds $2,901/month. PACE providers can also help coordinate this process.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her current doctors if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE becomes the sole provider for all covered services. Your mom will get a new primary care physician and specialist team through PACE, but this means one coordinated care team manages everything instead of juggling separate providers.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different county in Florida?
faqs.answer: PACE is only available in Miami-Dade and Broward counties. If your mom moves outside these areas, she would need to disenroll from PACE and return to regular Medicare/Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: How is this different from regular home health care?
faqs.answer: PACE provides comprehensive care including adult day programs, transportation, meals, all medical care, and emergency services — not just home visits. Because it covers everything under one program, there's no coordinating between different agencies or worrying about coverage gaps.

faqs.question: What if Mom needs nursing home care later?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing facility care when needed. Because your mom is already enrolled and the team knows her care needs, transitions to higher levels of care are coordinated by the same PACE team rather than starting over with new providers.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP)
programs.tagline: Help paying Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs for parents on fixed incomes.
programs.intro: If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays, Medicare Savings Programs can help. These four programs pay some or all Medicare costs based on income — from covering just the $185/month Part B premium to paying all Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs. Florida recently expanded eligibility by counting all household members (not just the applicant and spouse), significantly raising income limits for families living together. Even if your parent's income seems too high, certain income types don't count toward the limit — you should apply regardless.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premium plus potential deductibles and copayments

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local county Department of Children & Families office — processing time varies by county.

steps.title: Find your county DCF office
steps.description: Locate your local Department of Children & Families office at myflfamilies.com or call for the nearest location.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect recent bank statements, Medicare card, Social Security statements, and proof of any other income.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply in person, by phone, or by mail through your county office. Phone applications are accepted.

steps.title: Follow up on processing
steps.description: Contact your county office for processing timeline — this varies by location and current caseload.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program operates first-come, first-served and may have limited funding
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if income seems too high — many income types don't count toward the limit.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays all Medicare premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Covers Medicare Part B premium ($185/month in 2025)

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Covers Medicare Part B premium, first-come first-served basis

tiers.name: QDWI (Qualified Disabled Working Individual)
tiers.coverage: Helps pay Medicare Part A premiums only

contentSections.heading: QI Income Limits by Household Size (Highest Limits)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits. QMB and SLMB have lower income limits but same household size scaling.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Money in checking and savings accounts

contentSections.text: QI program funding is limited and operates first-come, first-served. Apply early in the year to secure coverage.
Florida recently changed how household size is calculated, making more families eligible. If you were previously denied, reapply — you may now qualify with the expanded household counting rules.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's home is completely exempt from asset limits, regardless of value. They can own a million-dollar home and still qualify if their liquid assets (bank accounts, investments) are under $9,950 for a single person or $14,910 for a couple.

faqs.question: What if Mom's Social Security is just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: Apply anyway. A $20 general exclusion applies to unearned income, and certain other income types don't count toward the limit. Many people who appear over the limit actually qualify once exclusions are applied.

faqs.question: Which program should my parent apply for?
faqs.answer: Apply for the highest tier your parent qualifies for based on income. QMB provides the most coverage (all Medicare costs), while SLMB and QI only cover the $185/month Part B premium. The county office will determine which program fits your parent's situation.

faqs.question: Does this affect my parent's other benefits?
faqs.answer: MSP benefits generally don't count as income for other programs. In fact, having MSP can sometimes help qualify for or increase benefits from programs like SNAP (food assistance) through simplified eligibility rules.

faqs.question: How long does coverage last?
faqs.answer: You must reapply annually to maintain benefits. QI program requires yearly reapplication particularly because of its limited funding. Set a calendar reminder to reapply each year before coverage expires.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different county in Florida?
faqs.answer: Benefits continue, but you'll need to transfer your case to the new county's Department of Children & Families office. Contact the new county office as soon as possible after moving to avoid any gaps in coverage.
